content.text: File system tools give a model the ability to read, write, and list files — useful for agents that process documents, generate reports, or manage data pipelines. The central safety requirement is confinement: the model must only be able to access a specific sandbox directory, not arbitrary paths on the host filesystem. Path traversal attacks (../../etc/passwd) must be blocked before any I/O operation.

attrs.alt: File system tool sandbox model with path validation guard blocking traversal attempts
attrs.title: File System Tool Sandbox Model

content.text: The security rule is simple: resolve every path to its absolute real path with os.path.realpath(), then check that it starts with the sandbox base directory. If it does not, return PERMISSION_DENIED before touching the filesystem.
Every file system tool must run the same path check before any read or write. Centralise it in a single function so the check cannot be accidentally omitted from a new tool.

Reading a file returns its text content, truncated at a safe limit. Include line_count and truncated in the response so the model knows if it received a partial view and can decide whether to request a specific line range.

The list directory tool lets the model discover what files exist before attempting to read them. Return file names, types, and sizes — not full content. Limit depth to avoid returning thousands of entries for deep directory trees.

Write tools must apply the stricter ALLOWED_WRITE_EXTS allowlist. Require explicit overwrite confirmation for existing files — do not silently overwrite without the model acknowledging the file exists.
Same as write_file, file must exist

import pytest
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path
from unittest.mock import patch

@pytest.fixture
def sandbox(tmp_path):
    (tmp_path / "data").mkdir()
    (tmp_path / "data" / "report.txt").write_text("line1\nline2\nline3")
    with patch("mymodule.SANDBOX_BASE", tmp_path):
        yield tmp_path

def test_read_file_returns_content(sandbox):
    result = read_file("data/report.txt")
    assert result["content"] == "line1\nline2\nline3"
    assert result["total_lines"] == 3
    assert result["truncated"] is False

def test_read_file_blocks_traversal(sandbox):
    result = read_file("../../etc/passwd")
    assert result["error"] == "PERMISSION_DENIED"

def test_write_file_conflict_without_overwrite(sandbox):
    result = write_file("data/report.txt", "new content", overwrite=False)
    assert result["error"] == "CONFLICT"

def test_write_file_overwrites_with_flag(sandbox):
    result = write_file("data/report.txt", "replaced", overwrite=True)
    assert result["created"] is True
    assert (sandbox / "data" / "report.txt").read_text() == "replaced"

def test_write_file_blocks_disallowed_ext(sandbox):
    result = write_file("script.sh", "#!/bin/bash\nrm -rf /", overwrite=False)
    assert result["error"] == "PERMISSION_DENIED"

Appending is safer than overwriting for log-like files — the model extends content without risk of losing existing data. Require the file to already exist so the model cannot create arbitrary new files via append.

For agents processing large codebases or log archives, a search_in_file tool that runs a keyword scan avoids the model reading every file in full. Return matching lines with their line numbers so the model can follow up with a targeted read_file call using start_line.

Before reading a large file, the model benefits from knowing its size and line count. A stat_file tool returning these without content lets the model decide whether to read the whole file or use start_line + max_lines to read in sections.

The readable_without_truncation field is a convenience hint — when True the model knows a single read_file call will return the complete file; when False it should use stat_file output to plan a multi-call read with start_line offsets.

For agents with write access, log every file operation to a separate audit log outside the sandbox. This creates an immutable record of what the agent read and wrote, useful for debugging unexpected behaviour and for security reviews.

import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone

audit_log = logging.getLogger("file_tool_audit")
audit_log.setLevel(logging.INFO)
_handler = logging.FileHandler("/var/log/agent_file_audit.log")
_handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter("%(asctime)s %(message)s"))
audit_log.addHandler(_handler)

def write_file(path: str, content: str, overwrite: bool = False) -> dict:
    resolved, err = _safe_path(path, write=True)
    if err:
        audit_log.warning("DENIED write path=%r reason=%s", path, err["error"])
        return err
    # ... write logic ...
    result = _do_write(resolved, content, overwrite)
    if "error" not in result:
        audit_log.info(
            "WRITE path=%r bytes=%d overwrite=%s",
            path, result["written_bytes"], overwrite
        )
    return result
Resolve every user-supplied path with os.path.realpath() and assert it starts with the sandbox base directory — this is the only reliable defence against path traversal.
Maintain separate allowlists for reads (.txt/.csv/.json/.md/.yaml/.log/.py) and writes (.txt/.csv/.json/.md) — executable file types must never be writable.
For read_file, include total_lines, returned_lines, start_line, and truncated so the model knows whether it received the full file and how to paginate.
For write_file, return CONFLICT when a file already exists and overwrite is false — require the model to explicitly acknowledge the overwrite.
list_directory caps at 100 entries and sets truncated:true when more exist — protects against huge directories stalling the context.
Test path traversal explicitly — resolve your sandbox base with tmp_path from pytest fixtures and assert traversal attempts return PERMISSION_DENIED.
